title: Z-function and its calculation
theory:
Z-function - Algorithms for Competitive Programming

          Skip to content
        

  Last update:
  August 25, 2025 

      Translated
    

      From: e-maxx.ru
    

Z-function and its calculation
Suppose we are given a string  of length . The Z-function for this string is an array of length  where the -th element is equal to the greatest number of characters starting from the position  that coincide with the first characters of .
In other words,  is the length of the longest string that is, at the same time, a prefix of  and a prefix of the suffix of  starting at .
Note. In this article, to avoid ambiguity, we assume -based indexes; that is: the first character of  has index  and the last one has index .
The first element of Z-function, , is generally not well defined. In this article we will assume it is zero (although it doesn't change anything in the algorithm implementation).
This article presents an algorithm for calculating the Z-function in  time, as well as various of its applications.
Examples
For example, here are the values of the Z-function computed for different strings:

"aaaaa" - 
"aaabaab" - 
"abacaba" - 

Trivial algorithm
Formal definition can be represented in the following elementary  implementation.
vector<int> z_function_trivial(string s) {
    int n = s.size();
    vector<int> z(n);
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        while (i + z[i] < n && s[z[i]] == s[i + z[i]]) {
            z[i]++;
        }
    }
    return z;
}

We just iterate through every position  and update  for each one of them, starting from  and incrementing it as long as we don't find a mismatch (and as long as we don't reach the end of the line).
Of course, this is not an efficient implementation. We will now show the construction of an efficient implementation.
Efficient algorithm to compute the Z-function
To obtain an efficient algorithm we will compute the values of  in turn from  to  but at the same time, when computing a new value, we'll try to make the best use possible of the previously computed values.
For the sake of brevity, let's call segment matches those substrings that coincide with a prefix of . For example, the value of the desired Z-function  is the length of the segment match starting at position  (and that ends at position ).
To do this, we will keep the  indices of the rightmost segment match. That is, among all detected segments we will keep the one that ends rightmost. In a way, the index  can be seen as the "boundary" to which our string  has been scanned by the algorithm; everything beyond that point is not yet known.
Then, if the current index (for which we have to compute the next value of the Z-function) is , we have one of two options:

 -- the current position is outside of what we have already processed.
We will then compute  with the trivial algorithm (that is, just comparing values one by one). Note that in the end, if , we'll have to update the indices of the rightmost segment, because it's guaranteed that the new  is better than the previous .

 -- the current position is inside the current segment match .
Then we can use the already calculated Z-values to "initialize" the value of  to something (it sure is better than "starting from zero"), maybe even some big number.
For this, we observe that the substrings  and  match. This means that as an initial approximation for  we can take the value already computed for the corresponding segment , and that is .
However, the value  could be too large: when applied to position  it could exceed the index . This is not allowed because we know nothing about the characters to the right of : they may differ from those required.
Here is an example of a similar scenario:
 s = "aaaabaa" 
When we get to the last position (), the current match segment will be . Position  will then match position , for which the value of the Z-function is . Obviously, we cannot initialize  to , it would be completely incorrect. The maximum value we could initialize it to is  -- because it's the largest value that doesn't bring us beyond the index  of the match segment .
Thus, as an initial approximation for  we can safely take:
 z_0[i] = \min(r - i,\; z[i-l]) 
After having  initialized to , we try to increment  by running the trivial algorithm -- because in general, after the border , we cannot know if the segment will continue to match or not.

Thus, the whole algorithm is split in two cases, which differ only in the initial value of : in the first case it's assumed to be zero, in the second case it is determined by the previously computed values (using the above formula). After that, both branches of this algorithm can be reduced to the implementation of the trivial algorithm, which starts immediately after we specify the initial value.
The algorithm turns out to be very simple. Despite the fact that on each iteration the trivial algorithm is run, we have made significant progress, having an algorithm that runs in linear time. Later on we will prove that the running time is linear.
Implementation
Implementation turns out to be rather concise:
vector<int> z_function(string s) {
    int n = s.size();
    vector<int> z(n);
    int l = 0, r = 0;
    for(int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        if(i < r) {
            z[i] = min(r - i, z[i - l]);
        }
        while(i + z[i] < n && s[z[i]] == s[i + z[i]]) {
            z[i]++;
        }
        if(i + z[i] > r) {
            l = i;
            r = i + z[i];
        }
    }
    return z;
}

Comments on this implementation
The whole solution is given as a function which returns an array of length  -- the Z-function of .
Array  is initially filled with zeros. The current rightmost match segment is assumed to be  (that is, a deliberately small segment which doesn't contain any ).
Inside the loop for  we first determine the initial value  -- it will either remain zero or be computed using the above formula.
Thereafter, the trivial algorithm attempts to increase the value of  as much as possible.
In the end, if it's required (that is, if ), we update the rightmost match segment .
Asymptotic behavior of the algorithm
We will prove that the above algorithm has a running time that is linear in the length of the string -- thus, it's .
The proof is very simple.
We are interested in the nested while loop, since everything else is just a bunch of constant operations which sums up to .
We will show that each iteration of the while loop will increase the right border  of the match segment.
To do that, we will consider both branches of the algorithm:

In this case, either the while loop won't make any iteration (if ), or it will take a few iterations, starting at position , each time moving one character to the right. After that, the right border  will necessarily be updated.
So we have found that, when , each iteration of the while loop increases the value of the new  index.

In this case, we initialize  to a certain value  given by the above formula. Let's compare this initial value  to the value . We will have three cases:

We prove that in this case no iteration of the while loop will take place.
It's easy to prove, for example, by contradiction: if the while loop made at least one iteration, it would mean that initial approximation  was inaccurate (less than the match's actual length). But since  and  are the same, this would imply that  holds the wrong value (less than it should be).
Thus, since  is correct and it is less than , it follows that this value coincides with the required value .

In this case, the while loop can make a few iterations, but each of them will lead to an increase in the value of the  index because we will start comparing from , which will climb beyond the  interval.

This option is impossible, by definition of .

So, we have proved that each iteration of the inner loop make the  pointer advance to the right. Since  can't be more than , this means that the inner loop won't make more than  iterations.
As the rest of the algorithm obviously works in , we have proved that the whole algorithm for computing Z-functions runs in linear time.
Applications
We will now consider some uses of Z-functions for specific tasks.
These applications will be largely similar to applications of prefix function.
Search the substring
To avoid confusion, we call  the string of text, and  the pattern. The problem is: find all occurrences of the pattern  inside the text .
To solve this problem, we create a new string , that is, we apply string concatenation to  and  but we also put a separator character  in the middle (we'll choose  so that it will certainly not be present anywhere in the strings  or ).
Compute the Z-function for . Then, for any  in the interval , we will consider the corresponding value . If  is equal to  then we know there is one occurrence of  in the -th position of , otherwise there is no occurrence of  in the -th position of .
The running time (and memory consumption) is .
Number of distinct substrings in a string
Given a string  of length , count the number of distinct substrings of .
We'll solve this problem iteratively. That is: knowing the current number of different substrings, recalculate this amount after adding to the end of  one character.
So, let  be the current number of distinct substrings of . We append a new character  to . Obviously, there can be some new substrings ending in this new character  (namely, all those strings that end with this symbol and that we haven't encountered yet).
Take a string  and invert it (write its characters in reverse order). Our task is now to count how many prefixes of  are not found anywhere else in . Let's compute the Z-function of  and find its maximum value . Obviously, 's prefix of length  occurs also somewhere in the middle of . Clearly, shorter prefixes also occur.
So, we have found that the number of new substrings that appear when symbol  is appended to  is equal to .
Consequently, the running time of this solution is  for a string of length .
It's worth noting that in exactly the same way we can recalculate, still in  time, the number of distinct substrings when appending a character in the beginning of the string, as well as when removing it (from the end or the beginning).
String compression
Given a string  of length . Find its shortest "compressed" representation, that is: find a string  of shortest length such that  can be represented as a concatenation of one or more copies of .
A solution is: compute the Z-function of , loop through all  such that  divides . Stop at the first  such that . Then, the string  can be compressed to the length .
The proof for this fact is the same as the solution which uses the prefix function.

title: Monotonic Stack
Stack
theory: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last In First Out (LIFO) principle. It supports operations like push and pop to add and remove elements from the top of the stack.

title: Queue
theory: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the First In First Out (FIFO) principle. It supports operations like enqueue and dequeue to add and remove elements from the front of the queue.

title: Array
theory: An array is a linear data structure that stores elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. It supports operations like accessing, inserting, and deleting elements by index.

title: Linked List
theory: A linked list is a linear data structure that stores elements of the same type using pointers. It supports operations like adding, removing, and searching nodes.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; Node* next;} *head = nullptr;Node* newNode(int value) {return new Node{value, nullptr};}void insertAtHead(Node*& head, int value) {Node* newNode = newNode(value);newNode->next = head;head = newNode;}

title: Hash Table
theory: A hash table is a data structure that uses a hash function to map keys to values. It supports operations like insertion, deletion, and searching elements by key.

title: Graph
theory: A graph is a non-linear data structure that consists of nodes and edges. It supports operations like adding, removing, and traversing edges.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; vector<Node*> neighbors;} *head = nullptr;void addEdge(Node*& head, int src, int dest) {Node* newNode = new Node{dest, {}};newNode->neighbors.push_back(head);head->neighbors.push_back(newNode);}

title: Priority Queue
theory: A priority queue is a special type of queue where the element with the highest (or lowest) priority is always removed first. It supports operations like insertion, deletion, and retrieving the maximum (or minimum) element.

title: Monotonic Stack
theory: A monotonic stack is a stack data structure that maintains its elements in either increasing or decreasing order. It is commonly used to solve problems involving nearest greater/smaller elements, range contributions, and linear-time array processing.

title: Stack of Plates
theory: A stack of plates is a data structure that represents a stack using two stacks. Each stack can hold a fixed number of plates, and the top plate of each stack represents the topmost element.
code_blocks: struct Plate {int value; bool isEmpty = true;} *top = nullptr;bool push(int value) {Plate* newPlate = new Plate{value};newPlate->next = top;top = newPlate;return true;}

title: Trie
theory: A trie is a tree-like data structure that stores words or other strings and can be used for efficient string search. It supports operations like insertion, deletion, and prefix search.
code_blocks: struct Node {unordered_map<char, Node*> children; bool isWord = false;} *root = nullptr;void insert(const string& word) {Node* curr = root;for (char c : word) {if (!curr->children.count(c)) {curr->children[c] = new Node();} curr = curr->children[c];} curr->isWord = true;}

title: Union-Find
theory: A union-find data structure is used to manage a set of elements into disjoint subsets. It supports operations like finding the root element, and merging two subsets.
code_blocks: struct UnionFind {int *parent; int size; UnionFind(int n) {parent = new int[n]; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) parent[i] = i; size = n;} void find(int x) {return parent[x] == x ? x : parent[x] = find(parent[x]);} bool unionSet(int x, int y) {int rootX = find(x); int rootY = find(y); if (rootX == rootY) return false; parent[rootX] = rootY; size--; return true;}

title: Binary Search Tree (BST)
theory: A binary search tree is a special type of binary tree where each node has at most two child nodes. It supports operations like insertion, deletion, and searching elements.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; struct Node* left; struct Node* right;} *root = nullptr;void insert(int value) {if (root == nullptr) {root = new Node{value};} else if (value < root->val) {insert(root->left, value);} else {insert(root->right, value);}}

title: Circular Linked List
theory: A circular linked list is a linear data structure where the last node's next pointer points back to the first node, creating a cycle. It supports operations like adding, removing, and traversing nodes.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; struct Node* next;} *head = nullptr;void insertAtTail(Node*& head, int value) {Node* newNode = new Node{value};if (head == nullptr) {head = newNode; head->next = newNode;} else {Node* curr = head;while (curr->next != head) {curr = curr->next;} curr->next = newNode;newNode->next = head;}

title: Deque
theory: A deque (double-ended queue) is a linear data structure that supports operations like insertion and removal from both ends. It is similar to a queue but allows elements to be added or removed at any end.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; struct Node* next; struct Node* prev;} *head = nullptr;void pushFront(int value) {Node* newNode = new Node{value};newNode->next = head;if (head != nullptr) {head->prev = newNode;} head = newNode;}

title: Red-Black Tree
theory: A red-black tree is a self-balancing binary search tree where each node has a color property (red or black) and satisfies certain properties. It supports operations like insertion, deletion, and searching elements.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; enum Color {RED, BLACK} color = RED;} *root = nullptr;void insert(int value) {if (root == nullptr) {root = new Node{value}; root->color = BLACK;} else if (value < root->val) {insert(root->left, value);} else {insert(root->right, value);}}

title: Heap
theory: A heap is a binary tree-based data structure that supports operations like insertion and deletion of elements, ensuring efficient access to the maximum (or minimum) element.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val;} *root = nullptr;void insert(int value) {Node* newNode = new Node{value};if (root == nullptr) {root = newNode;} else if (value < root->val) {insert(root->left, value);} else {insert(root->right, value);}}

title: Bloom Filter
theory: A bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure used to test whether an element is in a set. It uses hash functions and bit vectors to efficiently store and query membership.
code_blocks: struct BloomFilter {vector<bool> bits; int m; void insert(int value) {for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {bits[hash(i, value)] = true;}} bool contains(int value) {for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {if (!bits[hash(i, value)]) return false;} return true;}

title: Trie with Trie Prefix Tree
theory: A trie (prefix tree) is a special type of trie where each node stores a prefix and supports operations like insertion, deletion, and searching for prefixes.
code_blocks: struct Node {char val; unordered_map<char, Node*> children; bool isPrefix = false;} *root = nullptr;void insert(const string& word) {Node* curr = root;for (char c : word) {if (!curr->children.count(c)) {curr->children[c] = new Node{c};} curr = curr->children[c];} curr->isPrefix = true;}

title: Segment Tree
theory: A segment tree is a data structure that allows for efficient range queries and updates on an array. It supports operations like building, querying, and updating elements.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; int start; int end;} *root = nullptr;void build(Node*& root, int arr[], int start, int end) {if (start == end) {root->val = arr[start]; root->start = start; root->end = end;} else {int mid = (start + end) / 2; Node* left = new Node{};Node* right = new Node{};build(left, arr, start, mid); build(right, arr, mid + 1, end); root->val = max(left->val, right->val); root->start = start; root->end = end;} return root;}

title: Merge Sort
theory: Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that divides the input array into two halves, sorts each half, and then merges them back together.
code_blocks: void merge(int arr[], int low, int mid, int high) {int i = low; int j = mid + 1; int k = low; while (i <= mid && j <= high) {if (arr[i] < arr[j]) {arr[k++] = arr[i++];} else {arr[k++] = arr[j++];}} while (i <= mid) {arr[k++] = arr[i++];} while (j <= high) {arr[k++] = arr[j++];}} void mergeSort(int arr[], int low, int high) {if (low < high) {int mid = (low + high) / 2; mergeSort(arr, low, mid); mergeSort(arr, mid + 1, high); merge(arr, low, mid, high);}}

title: Quick Sort
theory: Quick sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that selects a pivot element from the array and partitions the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot.
code_blocks: void quickSort(int arr[], int low, int high) {if (low < high) {int pi = partition(arr, low, high); quickSort(arr, low, pi - 1); quickSort(arr, pi + 1, high);} void partition(int arr[], int low, int high) {int i = (low - 1); int pivot = arr[high]; for (int j = low; j < high; j++) {if (arr[j] <= pivot) {i++; swap(arr[i], arr[j]);}} swap(arr[i + 1], arr[high]); return i + 1;}

title: Binary Search
theory: Binary search is an efficient algorithm for searching a sorted array. It repeatedly divides the search interval in half until it finds the target or determines that the target is not present.
code_blocks: int binarySearch(int arr[], int low, int high, int key) {if (high >= low) {int mid = (low + high) / 2; if (arr[mid] == key) return mid; if (key < arr[mid]) return binarySearch(arr, low, mid - 1, key); else return binarySearch(arr, mid + 1, high, key);} return -1;}

title: Depth-First Search (DFS)
theory: Depth-first search is a recursive traversal algorithm that explores all the nodes of a graph or tree by traversing its adjacent nodes at the current level before moving to the next.
code_blocks: void dfs(vector<vector<int>>& graph, int u, vector<bool>& visited) {visited[u] = true; for (int i : graph[u]) {if (!visited[i]) dfs(graph, i, visited);} }

title: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
theory: Breadth-first search is an iterative traversal algorithm that explores all the nodes of a graph or tree by visiting each level of nodes before moving to the next.
code_blocks: void bfs(vector<vector<int>>& graph, int s) {vector<bool> visited(graph.size(), false); queue<int> q; q.push(s); visited[s] = true; while (!q.empty()) {int u = q.front(); q.pop(); for (int v : graph[u]) {if (!visited[v]) {visited[v] = true; q.push(v);} }}

title: Graph Theory
theory: Graph theory is the study of graphs, which are mathematical structures that consist of vertices (also called nodes) and edges connecting pairs of vertices.
code_blocks: struct Node {int val; vector<Node*> neighbors;} *head = nullptr;void addEdge(int value) {Node* newNode = new Node{value};newNode->next = head;if (head != nullptr) {head->prev = newNode;} head = newNode;}

title: Topological Sort
theory: Topological sort is an algorithm that can be used to determine the ordering of tasks in a project or process, where certain tasks must be completed before others.
code_blocks: void topologicalSort(int n, vector<vector<int>>& graph) {vector<int> indegree(n, 0); queue<int> q; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {for (int j : graph[i]) {indegree[j]++;}} for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {if (indegree[i] == 0) q.push(i);} vector<int> topologicalOrder; while (!q.empty()) {int u = q.front(); q.pop(); topologicalOrder.push_back(u); for (int v : graph[u]) {indegree[v]--; if (indegree[v] == 0) q.push(v);} }}

title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
theory: Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that finds the shortest path from a given source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph.
code_blocks: void dijkstra(int n, vector<vector<int>>& graph) {vector<int> dist(n, INT_MAX); dist[0] = 0; priority_queue<pair<int, int>> pq; pq.push({0, 0}); while (!pq.empty()) {int u = pq.top().second; pq.pop(); for (int v : graph[u]) {if (dist[v] > dist[u] + graph[u][v]) {dist[v] = dist[u] + graph[u][v]; pq.push({dist[v], v});}}}

title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
theory: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm is a dynamic programming algorithm that finds the shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph.
code_blocks: void floydWarshall(int n, vector<vector<int>>& graph) {vector<vector<int>> dist(n, vector<int>(n, INT_MAX)); for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) dist[i][i] = 0; for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {if (dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]) dist[i][j] = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j];} }

title: Kadane's Algorithm
theory: Kadane's algorithm is a dynamic programming algorithm that finds the maximum sum contiguous subarray within an array.
code_blocks: int maxSubArray(int arr[], int n) {int max_so_far = INT_MIN, max_ending_here = 0; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {max_ending_here += arr[i]; if (max_ending_here > max_so_far) max_so_far = max_ending_here;} return max_so_far;}

title: Two Pointers
theory: Two pointers is a technique that uses two indices moving through an array or string to solve problems efficiently in linear time. It is commonly used for sorted arrays, pair finding, partitioning, and sliding comparisons.

theory: Prefix sum is a preprocessing technique where cumulative sums are stored to answer range sum queries efficiently. It is commonly used in subarray and range query problems.

title: Backtracking
theory: Backtracking is a recursive search technique that builds solutions incrementally and abandons invalid paths early. It is used for permutations, combinations, subsets, and constraint satisfaction problems.

title: Dynamic Programming
